title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Role of Philosophy in Modern Education

texts.A: In an increasingly tech-driven world, philosophy is often viewed as a fossilized relic of a bygone era. This is a tragic misunderstanding. Philosophy is the 'operating system' of the human mind; it teaches us how to analyze logic, detect fallacies, and construct coherent arguments. Without these skills, students are defenseless against the sophisticated manipulation of modern advertising and political propaganda. We should make 'critical inquiry' a mandatory subject from primary school onwards. A society that can code but cannot think is a society in peril.
texts.B: The practical utility of a philosophy degree in the current labor market is undeniably low. While the 'soft skills' it provides are valuable, they can be acquired more efficiently through subjects like law, history, or even advanced computer science. We should stop encouraging students to take on massive debt for a subject that offers no clear career path. Education must be aligned with the economic needs of the nation, and that means prioritizing STEM over abstract metaphysical speculation. Philosophy belongs in the personal library, not the taxpayer-funded classroom.
texts.C: I agree that the 'job-readiness' argument against philosophy is a powerful one, but it assumes that the only purpose of education is to create a compliant workforce. I share the view that philosophy is essential for democracy; it is the only discipline that asks 'why' instead of just 'how.' However, I disagree with the idea that it should be a standalone subject. Philosophy is most effective when it is integrated into other fields—discussing the ethics of AI in a computer science class or the morality of markets in an economics lecture.
texts.D: The modern obsession with STEM has led to a crisis of meaning among the youth. Philosophy provides the tools for individuals to construct their own ethical frameworks in a secular world. While I share the skepticism regarding the 'marketability' of a pure philosophy degree, I believe the core concepts of logic and ethics are more 'practical' than many technical skills that become obsolete within a decade. Learning to think clearly is a 'future-proof' skill. We should prioritize the development of the person over the production of a worker.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the 'practicality' of philosophical skills?
questions.explanation: Expert B says philosophy has 'undeniably low' utility and no career path. Expert D argues that logic and ethics are actually 'more practical' and 'future-proof' than many technical skills.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the necessity of philosophy for a healthy democracy?
questions.explanation: Expert A believes it protects against 'political propaganda.' Expert C agrees, stating that 'philosophy is essential for democracy.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D regarding the limitations of a purely technical education?
questions.explanation: Expert D says 'learning to think clearly' is better than technical skills that become 'obsolete.' Expert A says a society that can 'code but cannot think is a society in peril.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on whether philosophy should be taught as an independent subject?
questions.explanation: Expert C argues against it being a 'standalone subject' and prefers it integrated. Expert A explicitly states we should make it a 'mandatory subject' (as its own entity) from primary school onwards.
